items.correct: Volume of air inhaled or exhaled in one normal breath at rest.
items.misconception: The maximum air your lungs can hold.
items.random_wrong: Air left in the lungs after maximum exhalation.
items.why: Students confuse tidal volume (resting breath, ~0.5L) with vital capacity (max breath, ~4-5L). Different lung volumes serve different purposes - tidal is the normal small ebb and flow.

items.q: How long does the ATP-PC system last at maximum intensity?
items.why: Students overestimate the ATP-PC system. It provides instant energy but stores deplete in ~10 seconds (one sprint, one heavy lift). After that, glycolysis or aerobic systems take over.

items.q: Which type of muscle is under voluntary control?
items.why: Students think all muscles can be controlled if we try hard enough. Cardiac and smooth muscle are involuntary - you can't consciously beat your heart. Only skeletal muscle responds to conscious commands.
It's 12 minutes. “1 minute” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: 20 m. “1 m” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many minutes is the Cooper test?
items.why: The real figure is 12. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
